message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Universal paid sick-leave (PSL) policies have been implemented in jurisdictions to mitigate the spread of SARS-CoV-2. However empirical data regarding health and economic consequences of PSL policies is scarce.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To estimate effects of a universal PSL policy in Ontario, Canada’s most populous province.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>An agent-based model (ABM) to simulate SARS-CoV-2 transmission informed by data from Statistics Canada, health administrative sources, and from the literature.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Ontario from January 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    to May 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2021.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A synthetic population (1 million) with occupation and household characteristics representative of Ontario residents (14.5 million).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A base case of existing employer-based PSL alone versus the addition of a 3-or 10-day universal PSL policy to facilitate testing and self-isolation among workers infected with SARS-CoV-2 themselves or because of infected household members.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome(s) and Measure(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Number of SARS-CoV-2 infections and COVID-19 hospitalizations, worker productivity, lost wages, and presenteeism (going to a workplace while infected).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>If a 3- and 10-day universal PSL were implemented over the 4-month study period, then compared with the base-case, the PSL policies were estimated to reduce cumulative SARS-CoV-2 cases by 85,531 (95% credible interval, CrI -2,484; 195,318) and 215,302 (81,500; 413,742), COVID-19 hospital admissions by 1,307 (-201; 3,205) and 3,352 (1,223; 6,528), numbers of workers forgoing wages by 558 (-327;1,608) and 7,406 (6,764; 8,072), and numbers of workers engaged in presenteeism by 24,499 (216; 54,170) and 279,863 (262,696; 295,449). Hours of productivity loss were estimated to be 10,854,379 (10,212,304; 11,465,635) in the base case, 17,446,525 (15,934,321; 18,854,683) in the 3-day scenario, and 26,127,165 (20,047,239; 29,875,161) in the 10-day scenario. Lost wages were $5,256,316 ($4,077,280; $6,804,983) and $12,610,962 ($11,463,128; $13,724,664) lower in the 3 day and 10 day scenarios respectively, relative to the base case.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Expanded access to PSL is estimated to reduce total numbers of COVID-19 cases, reduce presenteeism of workers with SARS-CoV-2 at workplaces, and mitigate wage loss experienced by workers.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Competing interests</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The authors have no competing interests relevant to this article to disclose.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Supported by COVID-19 Rapid Research Funding (C-291-2431272-SANDER). This research was further supported, in part, by a Canada Research Chair in Economics of Infectious Diseases held by Beate Sander (CRC-950-232429). The study sponsor had no role in the design, collection, analysis, interpretation of the data, manuscript preparation or the decision to submit for publication.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Author Contributions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Conceptualization: PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Data Curation: PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Formal Analysis: PP, JDR, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Methodology: PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Supervision: PP, DN, BS</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Validation: PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>First Draft: PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Review and Edit</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>PP, JDR, BS, DN</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>What could be the health and economic consequence of more generous paid sick leave policies in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>More generous policies are estimated to reduce SARS-CoV-2 infections (and thus COVID-19 hospitalizations), lost wages and presence of individuals with infection at workplaces.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>More generous paid sick leave can be a valuable addition to other COVID-19 public health interventions.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Health and Economic Consequences of Universal Paid Sick Leave Policies During the COVID-19 Pandemic
